headlines.title: PANTHERS WITH NOLLEY PITCHING BEAT NAVIGATORS
headlines.fullText:

headlines.title: T. C. U. PIGSKIN WARRIORS HERE EAGERFORCAMP
headlines.subtitle: PATTERSON IS RIX' SUCCESSOR AT UNIVERSITY
headlines.fullText:
The call of the pigskin has been an- wered. On nearly every train that en- tered Fort Worth Monday morning came one or more athletes to join the Texas Christian University football squad which will leave this afternoon for Venus, where a training camp will
One of the arrivals at T. C. U. Mon- day was Captain Reeder of Amarillo. Reeder is very optimistic over the pros- pects for a winning team. Severa promising men accompanied Reeder here from Amarillo. The camp at Venus will be in charge of Coach S. A. Boles, who last year Here squad. Assisting Boles will be F. M. Cahoon, who guided last year's Cheletian eleven
Manager Hooper has been busily en- caged lining up some of the promising succeeded Fort Worth material and has agencedec the 1912 Polytechnic College team will fly the T. C. U. colors this fall. Miller is one of the beat punters in the state and is sure to prove a valuable mana. From Central High, Hooper has been fortunate ta landing with bartin. Other arrivals Monday were Bradford and Anderson from Sherman High, and Whatley and Mayes from Mineral Wells. Rupley, of last year's Bryant team, will also leave with the squad Ramsay, last year's quarterback at 59. has trported along with Peter Wingo of the same team.
Cox, MeKnight, Golson and Barsler of the great T. C. br machine or tem who have reported ar Gerne at man. Parker, Broadley, Griffing. MeNamara, Reeder and Garrett.
The entire squad will leave over the L & G. N. for Venus this evening. Many other athletes will join the camy jater in the week, and by the time the Team Turma for regular scrimmage practice the squad will be composed of at least thirty-five men.

headlines.title: AMERICAN OFFICERS TO WATCH FIGHTING
headlines.subtitle: Austria and England Consent to Their Presence
headlines.fullText:
WASHINGTON, Aug. 31 .- Three American army officers to go to Aus- two officers to go with the British army were designated today by the war de- partment. Maj. Joseph H. Ford of the medical corps, now in London: Capt Augustine Mcintyre of the field artil- Tennessee, and Capt. Kerkley E. Nochs of the infantry, at present In Germany, CARL W. A. Cast
Castle of the Infantry Eleventh cavalry, both at present in Paris, have been designated to accom- pany the British troops
Germany, Russia and France so fat have not consented to have American observers accompany their armies.

headlines.title: GOLFERS LINE UP FOR TEE OFF IN AMATEUR TOURNEY
headlines.fullText:
MANCHESTER, VL, Ang. 31 .- More than a hundred of the leading golf players of the United States came to the links of the Ekwanok Country Club today for the opening the the thirtieth national amateur championship. Among the prominent partielpanta were Jerome D. Travers of Montclair, the present champion: Francie Ouimet of Wood- jane, last year's champion, and Charles Evans, Jr., of Edgewater. Today's play was an eighteen-hole medal qualifying round
Travers was scheduled to tee-off at 11:05, Outmet at 12:10 and Evans af 12:20.

headlines.title: Boxing Followers Look for Big Treat at Dallas
headlines.subtitle: Heavyweights Who Will Furnish Feature Entertainment Rank With Country's Best.
headlines.fullText:
Occasionally lovers of the boxing pastime are afforded opportunity to witness the scientific possibilities of the game and It is such an opportunity as this that will be offered when "Denver Jack" Geyer of Colorado meets Carl Mortis in a heavyweight clash in Dallas Fri- day, Sept. 4. It is assured by those who have watched Gever in recent aes game by any means. "Gever knows how to lead fast and back away from the clinch without being damaged, and that is about half that is Inciting in the present day aspirants for titles These young boxers make the mis- take of thinking that boxing is purely a slugging match. when it is nothing can wear the slugger out una decision without exerting ==== before him."
One of Morrie' earlier faults was lack of the fine points of the science, but he has since developed into a "shadow worker" difficult to reach and more difficult to stave off when he andre offensive. Geyer in one of his first
For a ble man he abenteuern: en" Morris will find that if the Colo- mãonn so decides he will have to car- ry the argument to him the greater part at the way.
Beth men have built and a punch, but they also are fast. It is seldom that the combination has been found en
Fortunate enenyh to claim the world's championship. James J. Corbett was among the very few scientific heavy- welents.
Recently at Denver, during a theatrical engagement, Corbett boxed a Few rounds with Geyer at the Denver Ath- letic Club. "He had me guessing for three rounds," said Corbett, "and he is by no means an easy man to hit. He knows how to side-step and cover under the swiftest kind of an attack. He is a hard hitter and a fast worker. He is one of the few men who has a chance to win the world's championship."
Morris is training for just the sort of an attack he expects from Geyer and white he may lacit a few of the riner points of the science that Geyer has. he will offer the sturdiest figure Geyer has encountered in recent months. Both men were anxious for the match, and each is determined that the result of It shall fare mo dogos as to who Is entitled to the firet con- sideration for a match with the world's champion. Both men regard the Dallas contest as the one real test of the year.

headlines.title: Leaflets to Incite Chinese.
headlines.fullText: PEKIN, Aug. 11-Leaflets based on alleged statements in Japanese newt. papers that Japan Intends to make China a dependency were distributed in the streets of Pekin yesterday.
